feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: A simple, whitespace theme for academics. Based on [*folio](https://github.com/bogoli/-folio) design.

entry.title: Phonon dispersion calculation for Graphene using QE

link.title: Phonon dispersion calculation for Graphene using QE

entry.content:
In Quantum Espresso, phonon dispersion is computed using the ph.x program, which is based on Density Functional Perturbation Theory.

(1) Perform SCF calculation to obtain the self-consistent electronic ground state energy using pw.x.

  • SCF input file (scf.graphene.in)
&control
  calculation = 'scf'
  restart_mode = 'from_scratch'
  pseudo_dir = './pseudos/'
  outdir = './out/'
  prefix = 'graphene'
/
&system
  ibrav = 4
  celldm(1) = 4.654
  celldm(3) = 6.0
  nat = 2
  ntyp = 1
  ecutwfc = 60.0
  ecutrho = 600.0
  assume_isolated = '2D'
/
&electrons
  conv_thr = 1.0e-8
/
ATOMIC_SPECIES
  C 12.0107 C.pbe-n-kjpaw_psl.1.0.0.UPF
ATOMIC_POSITIONS alat
  C 0.00000 0.00000 0.00000
  C 0.00000 0.57735 0.00000
K_POINTS {automatic}
  8 8 1 0 0 0
  • Execute SCF calculation
mpirun -np 4 pw.x -i scf.grap.in > scf.grap.out

(2) Compute the dynamical matrix on a uniform grid of q-points using ph.x.

  • Input file (ph.graphene.in)
&INPUTPH
  tr2_ph = 1.0d-15
  amass(1) = 12.0107
  ldisp = .true.
  nq1 = 6
  nq2 = 6
  nq3 = 1
  outdir = './out/'
  prefix = 'graphene'
  fildyn = 'graphene.dyn'
/
  • Perform the computation
mpirun -np 4 ph.x -i ph.graphene.in > ph.graphene.out
  • Output files

graphene.dyn0 contains coordinates of the q-point grid.

graphene.dyn1-N contains the force constants in $\boldsymbol q$-space.

(3) Perform inverse Fourier transform of the dynamical matrix to obtain the real-space force constants using q2r.x.

  • Input file (q2r.graphene.in)
&INPUT
  fildyn = 'graphene.dyn'
  zasr = 'simple'
  flfrc = 'graphene661.fc'
/
  • Execute the calculation
mpirun -np 4 q2r.x -i q2r.graphene.in > q2r.graphene.out
  • Output file

Si661.fc contains the interatomic force constants in real space for a 661 supercell.

(4) Perform Fourier transform of the real-space components using matdyn.x to obtain the dynamical matrix at any q-point.

  • Input file (matdyn.graphene.in)
&INPUT
  asr = 'simple'
  flfrc = 'graphene661.fc'
  flfrq = 'graphene.freq'
  q_in_band_form = .true.
  loto_2d = .true.
/
4
0.00000000 0.00000000 0.0   80
0.00000000 0.57735027 0.0   40
0.33333333 0.577

35027 0.0   60
0.00000000 0.00000000 0.0   1
  • Execute the calculation
mpirun -np 4 matdyn.x -i matdyn.graphene.in > matdyn.graphene.out

Then, visualize the results using the generated graphene.freq.gp file.
entry.summary: This method is based on Density Functional Perturbation Theory.

entry.title: Thermal conductivity calculation using QE+phono3py
entry.content:
Phono3py is a software for calculating phonon-phonon interactions and related properties using supercell method.

1. Read the input.in file and create a supercell

phono3py --qe -d --dim="4 4 1" -c graphene.in

The **supercell-xxx.in** file obtained here only contains the lattice structure of the supercell, which needs to be combined with the **header.in** file in the next step to form a new input file.

2. Use QE to perform self-consistent field (SCF) calculations

运行下列 **pwrun.sh** 文件，对不同位移下的所有超胞 **graphene-xxx.in** 文件进行 scf 计算

#！/bin/bash

for i in {00001..00170};
do
	cat header.in supercell-$i.in > input/graphene-$i.in;
	mpirun -np 4 pw.x input/graphene-$i.in > output/graphene-$i.out
done

3. Use phono3py to collect forces on atoms in QE calculation results ‘FORCE_FC3’

phono3py --qe --cf3 output/graphene-{00001..00170}.out

4. Calculate third-order and second-order force constants

phono3py --sym-fc

5. Calculate lattice thermal conductivity

phono3py --mesh="11 11 1" --fc3 --fc2 --br

In [1]: import h5py
In [2]: f = h5py.File("kappa-m111111.hdf5")
In [3]: list(f)
Out[3]:
['frequency',
'gamma',
'group_velocity',
'gv_by_gv',
'heat_capacity',
'kappa',
'kappa_unit_conversion',
'mesh',
'mode_kappa',
'qpoint',
'temperature',
'weight']
entry.summary: The calculation is based on the supercell method.

entry.title: Nanoheat
entry.content:
Macroscopic Energy Conservation Equation in Heat Transfer

The macroscopic energy conservation equation in heat transfer describes the storage, transfer (thermal conduction $k$, thermal convection $u$, and thermal radiation $r$), and transformation of thermal energy into other forms.

1. Energy Conservation

\[ abla\cdot oldsymbol{q} = - ho c_p rac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \sum_{i,j} \dot{s}_{i-j},\] \[ abla\cdot oldsymbol{q} = rac{\int_{\Delta A} ( oldsymbol{q} \cdot oldsymbol{s}_{ oldsymbol{n}}) dA}{\Delta V o 0}.\]

Here, $ ho c_p rac{\partial T}{\partial t}$ represents sensible heat storage, and $\dot{s}_{i-j}$ is the energy conversion rate of thermal energy determined by the nature and frequency of interactions between energy carriers $i$ and $j$.

2. Heat Flux Vector

\[ oldsymbol{q} = oldsymbol{q}_k + oldsymbol{q}_u + oldsymbol{q}_r\]

The heat flux vector $ oldsymbol{q}$ is the sum of conduction, convection, and radiation heat flux vectors.

Conduction Heat Flux Vector

\[ oldsymbol{q}_k = -k abla T\]

The conduction heat flux vector $ oldsymbol{q}_k$ is the product of the thermal conductivity $k$ and the negative temperature gradient $ abla T$, according to Fourier’s law of heat conduction.

Convection Heat Flux Vector

\[ oldsymbol{q}_u = ho c_p oldsymbol{u} T\]

The convection heat flux vector $ oldsymbol{q}_u$ is the product of $ ho c_p$, the local velocity vector $ oldsymbol{u}$, and the temperature $T$.

Radiation Heat Flux Vector

\[ oldsymbol{q}_r = 2\pi \int_0^\infty \int_{-1}^1 oldsymbol{s} I_{ph,\omega} d\mu d\omega\]

The radiation heat flux vector $ oldsymbol{q}r$ is the integral of the product of the unit vector $ oldsymbol{s}$ and the directional radiative intensity $I{ph,\omega}$ across space and the electromagnetic spectrum.

Major Energy Carriers

Four types of energy carriers—phonons ($p$), electrons ($e$), fluid particles ($f$), and photons ($ph$)—form the microscopic model of thermal energy storage, transfer, and interactions.

1. Phonons

  • Phonons are quantized vibrational modes in a rigid atomic lattice.
  • Long-wavelength phonons give rise to sound in solids, hence the name “phonons.”
  • Phonons determine many physical properties of materials, including heat capacity and thermal/electrical conductivity (phonon propagation governs heat conduction in insulators).
  • In classical mechanics, any lattice vibration can be decomposed into a superposition of nonlocal normal vibrational modes.
  • Quantum mechanically, these modes exhibit particle-like properties (wave-particle duality). Thus, phonons are quasiparticles.
  • Phonons are bosons with zero spin, and their energy $E_p = \hbar \omega_p$ is the sum of potential and kinetic energy, where $\hbar = h/2\pi$ and $h$ is Planck’s constant.
  • Two types of phonons:
    • Acoustic phonons (A): Frequency decreases with increasing wavelength, corresponding to sound waves in the lattice. Subtypes include longitudinal (LA) and transverse (TA) phonons.
    • Optical phonons (O): Occur in lattices with multiple atoms per unit cell and are easily excited by light. Subtypes include longitudinal (LO) and transverse (TO) optical phonons.

2. Electrons

  • Electrons are negatively charged subatomic particles with spin 1/2 (fermions).
  • Their antiparticles are positrons, which have the same mass and spin but opposite charge.
  • Electrons exhibit both particle and wave properties and can be considered quasiparticles. Electrons bound to the nucleus manifest as standing waves.
  • In solids, electrons are classified into:
    • Core electrons: Do not participate in bonding and move with the nucleus.
    • Valence electrons: Include conduction electrons (free electrons) and those in the outer shell.
  • Described quantum mechanically by the Dirac equation.
  • Electron energy comprises potential energy (bond energy) and kinetic energy (dependent on velocity).

3. Fluid Particles

  • Gases and liquids consist of atoms or molecules (collectively referred to as fluid particles) in random motion.
  • Fluid particle energy includes potential, electronic, and kinetic energy.
  • For an ideal gas, particle collisions are elastic, and intermolecular forces are negligible.
  • Ideal gas behavior is described by the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, derived using statistical mechanics.

4. Photons

  • Photons are quanta of the electromagnetic field and fundamental particles in quantum electrodynamics.
  • Photons exhibit wave-like properties and have zero rest mass but finite energy. They are subject to gravity due to their energy, according to general relativity.
  • Photons are bosons with spin 1 and are circularly polarized.
  • Energy of a photon:
\[E_{ph} = \hbar \omega_{ph},\]

where $\omega_{ph} = 2\pi f_{ph}$ is the angular frequency, and $f_{ph}$ is the frequency.

  • Momentum of a photon:
\[ oldsymbol{p}_{ph} = \hbar oldsymbol{\kappa}_{ph},\]

where $ oldsymbol{\kappa}_{ph}$ is the wave vector.

Energy Distribution Functions

For systems with multiple particles, the observed macroscopic state (ensemble average) is determined by the probability of each microscopic state (energy distribution function) and the microscopic states (positions and momenta) of each particle:

\[\langle \phi angle = \sum_i f_i \phi_i.\]

Equilibrium Distribution Functions:

  1. Bose-Einstein Distribution (Phonons, Photons):
\[f_i^0 = rac{1}{\exp\left( rac{E_i}{k_B T} ight) - 1}.\]
  1. Fermi-Dirac Distribution (Electrons):
\[f_i^0 = rac{1}{\exp\left( rac{E_i - \mu}{k_B T} ight) + 1}.\]
  1. Maxwell-Boltzmann Distribution (Ideal Gas Molecules):
\[f_i^0 = rac{1}{\exp\left( rac{E_i}{k_B T} ight)}.\]

Contributions to Heat Transfer Physics

Heat, a form of energy manifested as molecular motion, is transferred through conduction (phonons, electrons, fluid particles), convection (fluid particles), and radiation (photons).

Key Contributions:

  • Boltzmann: Developed energy equipartition theory and introduced the Boltzmann constant.
  • Maxwell: Founded electromagnetic theory and contributed to gas kinetics and thermodynamics.
  • Planck: Discovered quantum energy quantization, proposing that energy is emitted/absorbed in discrete quanta.
  • Schrödinger: Established quantum wave mechanics and the Schrödinger equation.
  • Green & Kubo: Developed fluctuation-dissipation theory for transport coefficients.
  • Ziman: Applied variational principles to phonon transport properties.

Fundamental Constants and Scales

Boltzmann Constant:

\[k_B = 1.38065 imes 10^{-23} \, ext{J/K}.\]

Planck Constant:

\[h = 6.626069 imes 10^{-34} \, ext{J·s}, \quad \hbar = rac{h}{2\pi}.\]

Atomic Units:

  • Atomic length:
\[r_B = rac{4\pi\varepsilon_0\hbar^2}{m_e e_c^2} = 5.2918 imes 10^{-11} \, ext{m}.\]
  • Atomic time:
\[ au_a = rac{m_e r_B^2}{\hbar} = 2.4189 imes 10^{-17} \, ext{s}.\]
  • Atomic energy:
\[ rac{e_c^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r_B} = 4.3597 imes 10^{-18} \, ext{J} = 27.211 \, ext{eV}.\]
entry.summary: Macroscopic Energy Conservation Equation in Heat Transfer
